preamble: The phonetic transformation of the alveolar lateral approximant /l/ into the diphthong /au/ is a well-documented process of vocalization, where a consonant shifts to a vowel or semivowel. This typically occurs in syllable-coda positions, where the /l/ loses its alveolar articulation and becomes a back rounded vocoid, often a [u] or [w] sound, which then forms the second element of a diphthong with a preceding vowel.

examples.note: Latin 'caldu(m)' (warm) transformed into Old French 'chaud', where the /l/ vocalized to /u/, forming the diphthong /au/.
Latin 'bellu(m)' (beautiful) evolved into Old French 'beau', with the /l/ vocalizing to /u/ and forming part of the diphthong /e̯au̯/.
In many Brazilian Portuguese dialects, syllable-final /l/ vocalizes to a labial-velar approximant [w], forming a diphthong like /aw/ (e.g., 'mal' (bad) pronounced as /maw/).

sources: Sato, Y., & Gick, B. (2022). Lateral vocalization in Brazilian Portuguese. The Journal of the Acoustical Society of America, 152(1), A10.
Final L ('Dark L') | Portuguese (Portugal) Grammar. (2026, May 8). Elon.io.
